sections.subtitle: Recent Form
sections.text: Everton have shown mixed results in their last 5 matches, securing 2 wins, 2 draws, and suffering 1 loss, indicating inconsistent form but a knack for grinding out results. Leeds United, on the other hand, have been slightly more promising with 2 wins, 2 draws, and 1 loss, displaying attacking intent with goals in most games. This suggests a competitive encounter with both teams capable of scoring.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: In their last two head-to-head matches, each team has claimed a 1-0 victory, with Leeds winning the most recent clash in August 2025. Historically, matches between these sides are tight, with Everton having the edge in first-to-score stats in recent encounters (5/6). Expect a closely contested battle with limited goals.

sections.subtitle: Key Players to Watch
sections.text: For Everton, James Garner and Kiernan Dewsbury-Hall are in fine form, with high Sofascore ratings and recent goal contributions. Leeds will rely on Dominic Calvert-Lewin, who has 9 goals this season, ranking 4th in the league, to spearhead their attack. These players could be decisive in breaking the deadlock.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics
sections.text: Everton, sitting 11th with 32 points, have a solid defensive record, conceding only 25 goals (3rd least in the league), but struggle with possession (19th) and goal-scoring (16th). Leeds, positioned 16th with 25 points, have scored more (30 goals) but are leaky at the back with 37 conceded, indicating potential vulnerabilities Everton could exploit.

sections.subtitle: Tactical Insights
sections.text: Everton’s strength lies in aerial duels (4th in the league) and clearances (2nd), suggesting a physical approach that could counter Leeds’ attacking flair. Leeds often score late in games (27% of goals after the 76th minute), so Everton must stay focused until the end. Corners and cards are likely to be limited, based on recent team streaks.

sections.subtitle: Match Prediction
sections.text: Considering Everton’s home advantage, defensive solidity, and Sofascore’s predicted 53% chance of a home win, they are slight favorites. Leeds’ attacking threat could lead to a draw (27% probability), but their defensive frailties may cost them. A narrow Everton victory seems the most likely outcome.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Half-time overview
halftimeSections.text: At half-time, Leeds United lead Everton 1-0 at Hill Dickinson Stadium in Liverpool. The solitary goal came from James Justin in the 28th minute with a well-taken left-footed strike. Leeds have shown more clinical finishing and attacking intent, creating several big chances while Everton have struggled to convert their possession and territorial presence into meaningful opportunities. The first half saw Leeds dominate in terms of expected goals and shots on target, while Everton failed to register a single shot on target despite having a decent share of the ball.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attacking momentum and patterns
halftimeSections.text: The momentum in the first half largely favored Leeds United, especially during key spells where they pushed forward with purpose and created sustained pressure. Everton had their moments early on, showing intent with a series of attacking moves in the opening minutes. However, their momentum waned as Leeds took control around the midpoint of the half, culminating in the goal. After conceding, Everton tried to respond with brief attacking spells towards the end of the half, but Leeds consistently regained momentum with threatening advances into the final third.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key team statistics
halftimeSections.text: Leeds United have outperformed Everton in critical attacking metrics, creating multiple big chances, though they missed a couple as well. Their higher expected goals tally reflects a more potent threat in front of goal compared to Everton, who have struggled to generate quality chances. Everton, despite not being far behind in ball possession, have been unable to translate their entries into the final third and penalty area into tangible scoring opportunities. Defensively, Everton have shown resilience with a strong percentage of duels won, particularly in the air, which could be a platform to build on in the second half.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Standout performers
halftimeSections.text: For Leeds United, James Justin has been the standout performer, not only scoring the decisive goal but also contributing effectively in both attack and defense. Anton Stach has also been pivotal with his playmaking, registering an assist and creating big chances for his teammates. On the Everton side, players like James Garner and Iliman Ndiaye have shown glimpses of promise with their involvement in midfield and defensive contributions, but they lack the cutting edge upfront. Dwight McNeil and Nathan Patterson have also worked hard defensively but need more support in attack.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Tactical observations
halftimeSections.text: Leeds United have effectively exploited spaces in Everton’s defense, particularly through quick transitions and penetrating runs into the penalty area. Their ability to win fewer duels yet still create danger suggests a more direct and purposeful style of play. Everton, on the other hand, have relied heavily on aerial dominance and physicality but have been let down by a lack of creativity in the final third. Without sharper finishing or better chance creation, they risk falling further behind. Leeds’ missed big chances also indicate potential vulnerability if Everton can capitalize on counter-attacks.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Second half expectations
halftimeSections.text: The second half is likely to see Everton push harder for an equalizer, potentially opening up spaces for Leeds to exploit on the counter. Given Everton’s inability to create big chances so far, they will need to take more risks, possibly leaving gaps at the back. Leeds, with their attacking momentum and clinical edge, are well-positioned to add to their tally if they maintain their composure. Players like James Justin could be key again for Leeds, while Everton will hope someone like James Garner or Thierno Barry can step up with a moment of quality to turn the tide.
